mentions.note: Consumer staples example used to illustrate sector rotation and confirmation bias pitfalls.
Historical example that residential solar was a financing story, not a renewables story; the group was crushed in 2023 once long-term rates stayed elevated.
Residential solar stock roared 2020–2022 on renewable energy narrative, crushed in 2023 when interest-rate sensitivity exposed; real driver was low-rate financing, not solar adoption.
Mentioned as consumer staples/recession-resistant stock alongside Colgate and Proctor; used for sector rotation illustration.
2021 SPAC EV play down 90%+ from peak; part of wave of speculative EV deals that collapsed when supply overwhelmed demand.
2021 SPAC EV play down 90%+ from peak; founder/CEO later sentenced to prison for fraud. Exemplifies IPO bubble dangers and SEC oversight gaps with SPACs.

Cited as the answer to a caller's Buffett question: perennially looks expensive on forward estimates but so consistently trumps them that in hindsight the stock was cheap — a pattern intact since 2012.
Buffett 'anchor' example: appears expensive on forward earnings but consistently beats estimates, making backward valuations cheap. Pattern since 2012; let it run.
Used as a teaching example, not a recommendation: a CPG recession stock whose rallies often reflect defensive sector rotation rather than company fundamentals.
Used as illustrative example of rotation risk; good fundamental company but stock may rally on sector rotation unrelated to P&G specifics.

Historical cautionary tale of SPAC-era speculation: peaked near $132 in December 2020 with no meaningful revenue, then collapsed to single digits by late 2022, with subsequent bounces attributed to short squeezes.
2020 SPAC merger EV battery play peaked ~$132 Dec 2020, collapsed to single digits by 2022; zero revenue, long way from commercialization. Emblematic of IPO-era excess.
2021 IPO EV play down 90%+ from peak; though bounced back, illustrates IPO cycle risk and supply glut dynamics.
2019 IPO that soared in 2020 when pandemic made platform essential; example of hot IPO that caught fire; used to illustrate IPO cycle exuberance.
